reasoning_summary: Run 20260421_194414_bf16_gemm_v1_ac1299d7 at 24.688641 ms says the branch is back on the true compact PTX anchor surface, but it is still a hair behind the accepted 24.682431 ms mark and remains capped at 201 regs/thread with `launch__occupancy_limit_registers = 2.0`. The recent search history already ruled out the obvious local sync trims, grouped-row retunes, and the current x32 staged branch, while the compact PTX loop body itself is now back to the clean wait/sync form. With only two rounds left, the best bounded unresolved probe is to keep the exact clean anchor logic and retune only the PTX microkernel launch-bounds target from 2 to 3 CTAs/SM. That is the narrowest remaining way to ask whether the branch can buy a little more latency hiding from register-pressure shaping without reopening a known-bad consume path or staging family. I rank an immediate restore of the 2-CTA clean anchor second as the safety fallback if the launch-bounds probe loses. I leave the old compact barrier-trim family third only as a parked ledger entry because the current anchor has already falsified the most plausible low-risk seam edits.
family_audit: accept: the branch is back on the clean compact PTX wait/sync anchor, so any new probe should preserve that logic unless it shows a clear win.
probe: the remaining unresolved local lever is the PTX microkernel launch-bounds target, because occupancy is still capped at 2 CTAs/SM with 201 registers per thread.
defer: grouped_rows, regular-sibling swaps, and x32 staged families already carry enough negative evidence on nearby surfaces that they should not outrank a one-line launch-bounds probe.

directions.name: Retune The Clean Compact PTX Launch Bounds To Target Three-CTA Residency
directions.hypothesis: The compact PTX anchor is now stable again, but it still sits at 201 regs/thread with occupancy limited to 2 CTAs/SM. Tightening only the PTX microkernel launch-bounds target from 2 to 3 may trim enough live state to improve latency hiding without changing grouped rows, stage depth, or the wait/sync cadence.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-pressure-driven CTA residency remains the cleanest unresolved local bottleneck on the restored compact PTX surface.
directions.risk: Moderate. The code edit is tiny, but forcing a tighter register budget can trade one latency problem for spills or a weaker instruction schedule.
ranking_notes: This is the narrowest unresolved probe left on the live anchor because it changes one compile-time constraint and nothing else.
It directly targets the standing `launch__occupancy_limit_registers = 2.0` signal without reopening families that already failed on nearby compact PTX surfaces.
Frontier should prefer this over stale restore candidates because the branch no longer needs recovery; it needs one last bounded local test.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if the next build or NCU run shows spill-driven fallout, registers stay flat while runtime regresses, or barrier/long_scoreboard both worsen.
expected_local_changes: Change only the compact PTX microkernel `__launch_bounds__(128, 2)` annotation to target 3 CTAs/SM.
Leave grouped_rows=4, the clean wait/sync cadence, stage depth, and dispatch routing unchanged.

directions.name: Restore The Two-CTA Clean Compact PTX Anchor If The Launch-Bounds Probe Loses
directions.hypothesis: If the 3-CTA launch-bounds probe loses, the correct action is an immediate rollback to the clean compact PTX anchor with `__launch_bounds__(128, 2)` rather than another speculative local tweak.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The fallback issue would be a failed register-budget probe rather than a new algorithmic bottleneck.
directions.risk: Low. This is a one-line restore to the current measured clean anchor.
ranking_notes: This stays second so the final round has an immediate recovery path if the launch-bounds probe fails.
The clean anchor is already measured and should be restored directly rather than mixing rollback with a new idea.
It ranks behind the probe because the branch is healthy enough for one bounded experiment before the loop closes.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if restoring the anchor would require anything beyond resetting the PTX microkernel launch-bounds annotation.
expected_local_changes: Reset the compact PTX microkernel launch-bounds target back to 2 if the probe loses.

directions.name: Keep The Compact Barrier-Trim Family Parked Behind The Launch-Bounds Probe
directions.hypothesis: The compact barrier-trim family still points at the right kind of hotspot, but the current branch has already measured the highest-confidence low-risk variants and they regressed. It should remain parked behind the one-line launch-bounds probe.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual synchronization cost on the compact PTX loop, but with weaker marginal evidence than the register-pressure lever.
directions.risk: Moderate. The family is local, but the recent evidence says it is more likely to churn than to improve.
ranking_notes: This remains on the board only to keep the search ledger honest about the remaining local family.
It ranks last because the current anchor already falsified the most plausible compact seam edits.
Do not spend round 19 on this family while the cleaner launch-bounds probe remains untested on the true anchor.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the proposal duplicates the refill-before-wait or final-no-refill-sync variants already measured on the compact anchor.
expected_local_changes: Keep the compact barrier-trim family parked unless the launch-bounds probe is exhausted.

notes: Round 19/20 diagnosis emitted from the restored clean compact PTX anchor; frontier should probe the PTX launch-bounds target before the loop closes.
